review_verdict.feedback: The plan accurately follows the logic provided in the recommendation to identify, stop, and mask the systemd-coredump.socket unit. It correctly uses masking to ensure the service cannot be inadvertently started.
suggested_improvements: The plan could be improved by also masking 'systemd-coredump.service' to ensure total coverage, though masking the socket is the primary requirement to prevent activation.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the recommended remediation to disable core dump processing by masking the systemd-coredump.socket unit. Masking a socket responsible for core dumps does not impact critical system services like sshd, networking, or firewalld. Disabling core dumps is a standard security hardening practice to prevent sensitive information from being written to disk during a crash.
side_effects: Loss of automatic core dump generation and storage for debugging purposes

vulnerability.title: Disable core dump backtraces
vulnerability.description: The ProcessSizeMax option in [Coredump] section of /etc/systemd/coredump.conf or in a drop-in file under /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/ specifies the maximum size in bytes of a core which will be processed. Core dumps exceeding this size may be stored, but the backtrace will not be generated.
vulnerability.recommendation:
Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q systemd; }; then found=false # set value in all files if they contain section or key for f in $(echo -n "/etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/complianceascode_hardening.conf /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/*.conf /etc/systemd/coredump.conf"); do if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then continue fi # find key in section and change value if grep -qzosP "[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]([^
\[]*
+)+?[[:space:]]*ProcessSizeMax" "$f"; then if ! grep -qPz "ProcessSizeMax=0" "$f"; then sed -i "s/ProcessSizeMax[^(
)]*/ProcessSizeMax=0/" "$f" fi found=true # find section and add key = value to it elif grep -qs "[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]" "$f"; then sed -i "/[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]/a ProcessSizeMax=0" "$f" found=true fi done # if s

triage.reason: The remediation involves modifying a systemd configuration file to limit the size of core dump backtraces. This is a standard hardening practice to prevent sensitive information leakage and does not impact system availability, networking, or authentication.
triage.estimated_impact: Minimal risk of impacting debugging capabilities for developers

vulnerability.title: Disable Core Dumps for All Users

vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q pam; }; then SECURITY_LIMITS_FILE="/etc/security/limits.conf" DROPIN_DIR="/etc/security/limits.d" DROPIN_FILE="$DROPIN_DIR/10-ssg-hardening.conf" REGEX_CORRECT_VALUE="^\s*\*\s+hard\s+core\s+0\s*$" # Remove bad configuration in drop-ins if [ -d "$DROPIN_DIR" ]; then for override in "$DROPIN_DIR"/*.conf; do if [ -f "$override" ] && ! grep -qE "$REGEX_CORRECT_VALUE" "$override"; then sed -ir -E '/^[[:space:]]*\*[[:space:]]+hard[[:space:]]+core[[:space:]]+/ s/^/#/' "$override" fi done fi if [ -d "$DROPIN_DIR" ] && grep -qEr "$REGEX_CORRECT_VALUE" "$DROPIN_DIR"; then exit 0 elif [ ! -d "$DROPIN_DIR" ] && grep -qE "$REGEX_CORRECT_VALUE" "$SECURITY_LIMITS_FILE"; then exit 0 else mkdir -p "$DROP

vulnerability.title: Enable Randomized Layout of Virtual Address Space
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.randomize_va_space kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.randomize_va_space = 2
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.randomize_va_space from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.randomize_va_space.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.randomize_va_space" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config f

triage.reason: Enabling Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) via sysctl is a standard security hardening practice that does not impact system availability or connectivity. The remediation involves a simple sysctl configuration change.

vulnerability.title: Enable page allocator poisoning
vulnerability.description: To enable poisoning of free pages, add the argument page_poison=1 to the default GRUB 2 command line for the Linux operating system. To ensure that page_poison=1 is added as a kernel command line argument to newly installed kernels, add page_poison=1 to the default Grub2 command line for Linux operating systems. Modify the line within /etc/default/grub as shown below: GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="... page_poison=1 ..." Run the following command to update command line for already installed kernels:# grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="page_poison=1" If the system is distributed as a bootable container image, GRUB2 can't be configured using the method described above, but the following method needs to be used instead. The kernel arguments should be set in /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d in a TOML file that has the following form: # /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/10-example.toml kargs = ["page_poison=1"] For more details on configuring kernel arguments in bootable container images, please refer to Bootc documentation.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q grub2-common; }; then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; } ; then KARGS_DIR="/usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/" if grep -q -E "page_poison" "$KARGS_DIR/*.toml" ; then sed -i -E "s/^(\s*kargs\s*=\s*\[.*)\"page_poison=[^\"]*\"(.*]\s*)/\1\"page_poison=1\"\2/" "$KARGS_DIR/*.toml" else echo "kargs = [\"page_poison=1\"]" >> "$KARGS_DIR/10-page_poison.toml" fi else grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args=page_poison=1 fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation involves modifying kernel command line arguments via GRUB or bootc. While this is a standard hardening practice, changes to the bootloader/kernel arguments carry an inherent risk of preventing the system from booting correctly if the syntax is malformed or if the specific kernel version has compatibility issues with the argument.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential for boot failure if GRUB configuration is corrupted; Kernel parameter incompatibility; Requires reboot to take effect

triage.reason: The finding requires the installation of a standard utility package via the package manager (dnf). Installing utility packages is a low-risk operation that does not modify existing configurations or disrupt system services.

vulnerability.title: Disable KDump Kernel Crash Analyzer (kdump)
vulnerability.description: The kdump service provides a kernel crash dump analyzer. It uses the kexec system call to boot a secondary kernel ("capture" kernel) following a system crash, which can load information from the crashed kernel for analysis. The kdump service can be disabled with the following command: $ sudo systemctl mask --now kdump.service
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" stop 'kdump.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" disable 'kdump.service' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" mask 'kdump.service' # Disable socket activation if we have a unit file for it if "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" -q list-unit-files kdump.socket; then if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" stop 'kdump.socket' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" mask 'kdump.socket' fi # The service may not be running because it has been started and failed, # so let's reset the state so OVAL checks pass. # Service should be 'inactive', not 'failed' after reboot though. "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" reset-failed

triage.reason: Disabling kdump is a standard hardening procedure to reduce the attack surface and resource usage. It involves masking a service and does not impact system availability, networking, or authentication.
triage.estimated_impact: Loss of kernel crash dump capabilities for debugging purposes

vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on cron.d
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/cron.d, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0700 /etc/cron.d
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then find -H /etc/cron.d/ -maxdepth 0 -perm /u+s,g+xwrs,o+xwrt -type d -exec chmod u-s,g-xwrs,o-xwrt {} \; else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The finding involves tightening directory permissions on /etc/cron.d to prevent unauthorized access or modification of scheduled tasks. This is a standard hardening practice and follows the provided policy for file permission changes.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential disruption of cron jobs if non-root users require access to this directory (highly unlikely for standard STIG compliance)

remediation.scan_output: Vulnerability file_permissions_cron_d not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The finding `file_permissions_cron_d` (Verify Permissions on cron.d) has been remediated.

**Summary of Actions:**
1.  **Triage:** Verified current permissions of `/etc/cron.d` (originally `drwxr-xr-x` owned by `root:root`).
2.  **Remediation:** Executed `chmod 0700 /etc/cron.d` to restrict directory access to the root user only.
3.  **Verification:** Confirmed new permissions are `drwx------` (0700) and ownership remains `root:root`.

vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/cron.daily, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0700 /etc/cron.daily
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then find -H /etc/cron.daily/ -maxdepth 0 -perm /u+s,g+xwrs,o+xwrt -type d -exec chmod u-s,g-xwrs,o-xwrt {} \; else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The finding involves tightening directory permissions on /etc/cron.daily to prevent unauthorized access or modification. This is a standard hardening practice for file permissions and does not impact system availability or authentication mechanisms.

remediation.scan_output: Vulnerability file_permissions_cron_daily not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation is complete. I have successfully changed the permissions of `/etc/cron.daily` to `0700` (drwx------), ensuring that only the root user can access the directory, which addresses the security finding.

**Summary of Actions:**
1.  **Inspected initial permissions**: `ls -ld /etc/cron.daily` showed `drwxr-xr-x`.
2.  **Applied fix**: Executed `chmod 0700 /etc/cron.daily`.

all_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability file_permissions_cron_daily not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation is complete. I have successfully changed the permissions of `/etc/cron.daily` to `0700` (drwx------), ensuring that only the root user can access the directory, which addresses the security finding.

**Summary of Actions:**
1.  **Inspected initial permissions**: `ls -ld /etc/cron.daily` showed `drwxr-xr-x`.
2.  **Applied fix**: Executed `chmod 0700 /etc/cron.daily`.
3.  **Verified fix**: `ls -ld /etc/cron.daily` confirmed the new permissions are `drwx------`.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will successfully address the vulnerability by setting the directory permissions to 0700. However, it follows the finding's specific command (0700) which is more restrictive than the recommendation's logic. While 0700 is secure, it may prevent system utilities or specific users from accessing the directory if they are not root, though typically only root needs access to cron.daily contents. The plan is pragmatic and effective.
concerns: Setting permissions to 0700 (drwx------) is very restrictive and might impact certain system management tools that expect standard directory permissions (e.g., 0755), although for /etc/cron.daily, root access is usually sufficient.
suggested_improvements: Consider if 0755 or 0750 is more appropriate for system compatibility, although 0700 satisfies the hardening requirement.

The remediation recommendation in the description uses a complex 'find' command with bitwise permission removal, whereas the plan uses an explicit 'chmod 0700'. The explicit 'chmod' is easier to verify but more aggressive.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by hardening the permissions of /etc/cron.daily. In RHEL/Rocky Linux, /etc/cron.daily is primarily managed and accessed by the cron daemon (running as root) and the root user. Setting permissions to 0700 (drwx------) ensures only the owner (root) can access the directory, which is a standard security hardening practice for sensitive system configuration directories. It is unlikely to disrupt system services or user access.
side_effects: Non-root users/tools will no longer be able to list or access the /etc/cron.daily directory

vulnerability.title: Enable the File Access Policy Service
vulnerability.description: The File Access Policy service should be enabled. The fapolicyd service can be enabled with the following command: $ sudo systemctl enable fapolicyd.service
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" unmask 'fapolicyd.service' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" start 'fapolicyd.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" enable 'fapolicyd.service' else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: While enabling a service is generally low risk, fapolicyd (File Access Policy Daemon) is a security framework that controls which applications are allowed to execute. If the policy is not correctly configured for the specific workload, enabling it can immediately block legitimate binaries, scripts, or services from running, potentially causing application failure or system instability.
triage.estimated_impact: May block execution of legitimate binaries not present in the trust database; Potential for application breakage if custom scripts or binaries are used

vulnerability.title: Configure Fapolicy Module to Employ a Deny-all, Permit-by-exception Policy to Allow the Execution of Authorized Software Programs.
vulnerability.description: The Fapolicy module must be configured to employ a deny-all, permit-by-exception policy to allow the execution of authorized software programs and to prevent unauthorized software from running.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then cat > /etc/fapolicyd/rules.d/99-deny-everything.rules << EOF # Red Hat KCS 7003854 (https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7003854) deny perm=any all : all EOF chmod 644 /etc/fapolicyd/rules.d/99-deny-everything.rules chgrp fapolicyd /etc/fapolicyd/rules.d/99-deny-everything.rules if [ -e "/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*permissive\s*=\s*/Id" "/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf" else touch "/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf" cp "/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf" "/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "permissive = 0" >> "/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf" # C

triage.reason: Implementing a 'deny-all' fapolicyd policy is highly disruptive. While it is a standard hardening pattern, applying it without first creating a comprehensive whitelist of all authorized binaries, libraries, and scripts will immediately break system functionality, prevent service execution, and potentially block administrative tools.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Immediate execution failure of unauthorized or unlisted binaries; Potential breakage of critical system services and cron jobs; Risk of breaking administrative access if shell or system tools are not explicitly permitted

vulnerability.title: Configure System to Forward All Mail For The Root Account
vulnerability.description: Make sure that mails delivered to root user are forwarded to a monitored email address. Make sure that the address is a valid email address reachable from the system in question. Use the following command to configure the alias: $ sudo echo "root: " >> /etc/aliases $ sudo newaliases
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_postfix_root_mail_alias='' # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^root") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s: %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_postfix_root_mail_alias" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m 1 -i -e "^root\\>" "/etc/aliases"; then escaped_formatted_output=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$formatted_output") LC_ALL=C sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^root\\

triage.reason: The remediation involves modifying /etc/aliases and running newaliases. While generally safe, the rule requires a valid, reachable email address to be specified. Automating this with an empty or incorrect address could result in lost system notifications. A human must provide the specific target email address.
triage.estimated_impact: Incorrect email address configuration could lead to loss of critical system alerts; Requires manual input of a valid destination email

vulnerability.title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
vulnerability.description: The maxpoll should be configured to in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) to continuously poll time servers. To configure maxpoll in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) add the following after each server, pool or peer entry: maxpoll to server directives. If using chrony, any pool directives should be configured too.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { ( rpm --quiet -q chrony || rpm --quiet -q ntp ); }; then var_time_service_set_maxpoll='' pof="/usr/sbin/pidof" CONFIG_FILES="/etc/ntp.conf" $pof ntpd || { CHRONY_D_PATH=/etc/chrony.d/ mapfile -t CONFIG_FILES < <(find ${CHRONY_D_PATH}.* -type f -name '*.conf') CONFIG_FILES+=(/etc/chrony.conf) } # get list of ntp files for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Set maxpoll values to var_time_service_set_maxpoll sed -i "s/^\(\(server\|pool\|peer\).*maxpoll\) [0-9,-][0-9]*\(.*\)$/\1 $var_time_service_set_maxpoll \3/" "$config_file" done for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Add maxpoll to server, pool or peer entries without maxpoll grep "^\(server\|pool\|peer\)" "$config_file" | grep -v maxpo

vulnerability.title: Disable Host-Based Authentication
vulnerability.description: SSH's cryptographic host-based authentication is more secure than .rhosts authentication. However, it is not recommended that hosts unilaterally trust one another, even within an organization. The default SSH configuration disables host-based authentication. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for HostbasedAuthentication. To explicitly disable host-based authentication, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: HostbasedAuthentication no
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*HostbasedAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*HostbasedAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*HostbasedAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "

triage.reason: Disabling host-based authentication is a standard SSH hardening practice. Since the default SSH configuration already disables this feature, applying this change reinforces the secure default and is unlikely to disrupt standard user authentication methods like password or public key authentication.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential disruption if the environment relies on legacy host-based authentication for automated machine-to-machine communication

vulnerability.title: Disable Compression Or Set Compression to delayed
vulnerability.description: Compression is useful for slow network connections over long distances but can cause performance issues on local LANs. If use of compression is required, it should be enabled only after a user has authenticated; otherwise, it should be disabled. To disable compression or delay compression until after a user has successfully authenticated, add or correct the following line in the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file: Compression
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_sshd_disable_compression='' mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*Compression\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*Compression\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*Compression\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" cp "/

triage.reason: Disabling SSH compression is a standard hardening practice to mitigate potential DoS vulnerabilities (like the 'CRIME' or 'BREACH' style attacks in certain contexts) and performance issues. The remediation involves a simple configuration change in sshd_config which is unlikely to cause lockout, provided the SSH service is restarted correctly.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential increase in bandwidth usage for high-latency/low-bandwidth connections

vulnerability.title: Disable Kerberos Authentication
vulnerability.description: Unless needed, SSH should not permit extraneous or unnecessary authentication mechanisms like Kerberos. The default SSH configuration disallows authentication validation through Kerberos. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for KerberosAuthentication. To explicitly disable Kerberos authentication, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: KerberosAuthentication no
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*KerberosAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*KerberosAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*KerberosAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/et

triage.reason: While disabling unnecessary authentication mechanisms is standard hardening, Kerberos is often a critical component for enterprise identity management and centralized authentication. Disabling it via SSH configuration could immediately lock out administrators or automated systems that rely on Kerberos for SSH access, especially in domain-joined environments.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential loss of SSH access for users relying on Kerberos authentication; Disruption of automated management tools using Kerberos-based SSH keys/tokens

vulnerability.title: Disable SSH Support for .rhosts Files
vulnerability.description: SSH can emulate the behavior of the obsolete rsh command in allowing users to enable insecure access to their accounts via .rhosts files. The default SSH configuration disables support for .rhosts. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for IgnoreRhosts. To explicitly disable support for .rhosts files, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: IgnoreRhosts yes
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complia

triage.reason: Disabling .rhosts support is a standard SSH hardening practice that mitigates an obsolete and insecure authentication method. The change involves adding a configuration directive to sshd_config, which does not impact modern SSH key-based or password-based authentication.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential loss of access for legacy systems relying on .rhosts (highly unlikely in modern environments)

triage.reason: Enabling public key authentication is a standard security hardening practice for SSH. The remediation involves adding a configuration file to the sshd_config.d directory, which is a non-destructive way to manage SSH settings. vulnerability.title: Enable Use of Strict Mode Checking
vulnerability.description: SSHs StrictModes option checks file and ownership permissions in the user's home directory .ssh folder before accepting login. If world- writable permissions are found, logon is rejected. The default SSH configuration has StrictModes enabled. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for StrictModes. To explicitly enable StrictModes in SSH, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: StrictModes yes
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*StrictModes\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*StrictModes\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*StrictModes\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-compliance

triage.reason: Enabling StrictModes is a standard SSH hardening practice that prevents logins if user configuration files are insecurely permissioned. Since the default is often already enabled or the change is a simple configuration addition, the risk of system breakage is extremely low, though users with incorrectly permissioned .ssh directories may experience login failures.

vulnerability.title: Enable SSH Print Last Log
vulnerability.description: Ensure that SSH will display the date and time of the last successful account logon. The default SSH configuration enables print of the date and time of the last login. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for PrintLastLog. To explicitly enable LastLog in SSH, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: PrintLastLog yes
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*PrintLastLog\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*PrintLastLog\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*PrintLastLog\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complia

triage.reason: The remediation involves adding a non-disruptive configuration directive (PrintLastLog yes) to the SSH daemon configuration. This is a standard hardening practice that enhances auditing/visibility without altering authentication mechanisms or access controls.

vulnerability.title: Force frequent session key renegotiation
vulnerability.description: The RekeyLimit parameter specifies how often the session key of the is renegotiated, both in terms of amount of data that may be transmitted and the time elapsed. To decrease the default limits, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: RekeyLimit
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_rekey_limit_size='' var_rekey_limit_time='' mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardenin

triage.reason: The change involves adjusting the RekeyLimit parameter in the SSH configuration to enforce more frequent key renegotiation. This is a standard security hardening practice for SSH and does not impact authentication mechanisms or connectivity, provided the SSH service is restarted to apply the changes.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential for minor increase in CPU overhead during high-volume data transfers due to more frequent rekeying

vulnerability.title: Set SSH Daemon LogLevel to VERBOSE
vulnerability.description: The VERBOSE parameter configures the SSH daemon to record login and logout activity. To specify the log level in SSH, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: LogLevel VERBOSE
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" cp "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode

triage.reason: Changing the SSH LogLevel to VERBOSE is a standard hardening practice to improve auditability. The remediation involves adding a configuration file in the sshd_config.d directory, which is a non-destructive way to manage SSH settings without modifying the main config file directly.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for increased log file size/disk usage due to more verbose logging

review_verdict.feedback: The plan is sound and directly addresses the previous review's concerns by including 'sshd -t' to validate configuration syntax before restarting the service. Using the sshd_config.d directory is a clean approach for managing compliance settings.
concerns: The command 'sed -i '/^\s*LogLevel/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf' may fail if no .conf files exist in that directory, depending on the shell's globbing behavior.
suggested_improvements: To make step 5 more robust, use a conditional check or ensure the glob matches at least one file to avoid shell errors.
Ensure a backup of /etc/ssh/sshd_config is created before running the sed command, just as a safety precaution.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the requirement to set the SSH LogLevel to VERBOSE. Importantly, it includes a syntax check using 'sshd -t' before attempting to restart the service, which is a critical safety measure to prevent SSH lockout due to configuration errors. Using the /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/ directory is the modern, recommended way to manage modular configurations on RHEL/Rocky Linux.
side_effects: Increased log file size due to more verbose logging.

vulnerability.title: Prevent remote hosts from connecting to the proxy display
vulnerability.description: The SSH daemon should prevent remote hosts from connecting to the proxy display. The default SSH configuration for X11UseLocalhost is yes, which prevents remote hosts from connecting to the proxy display. To explicitly prevent remote connections to the proxy display, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: X11UseLocalhost yes
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*X11UseLocalhost\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*X11UseLocalhost\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*X11UseLocalhost\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/0

triage.reason: This is a standard SSH hardening configuration change that ensures X11 forwarding is restricted to the local machine. It does not impact core SSH connectivity or authentication, only the security of X11 proxying.
triage.estimated_impact: May break X11 forwarding for remote users if they specifically require remote X11 access

The remediation attempt was unsuccessful due to a system-level restriction: the user account's password has expired, which prevents the execution of shell commands (as evidenced by the `stderr` message: `"You are required to change your password immediately (password expired)."`).

While I attempted to follow the approved plan to harden the SSH configuration by setting `X11UseLocalhost yes` in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf`, the commands failed to complete because the shell environment was interrupted by the password expiration policy.

**Attempted Remediation Steps:**
1.  **Directory Creation**: Created `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/`.
2.  **File Setup**: Created and set permissions (`0600`) for `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf`.
3.  **Conflict Removal**: Attempted to remove existing `X11UseLocalhost` entries from `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` using `sed`.
4.  **Configuration Application**: Attempted to write `X11UseLocalhost yes` to the drop-in file.
5.  **Validation & Activation**: Attempted to validate the SSH configuration syntax (`sshd -t`) and reload the service (`systemctl reload sshd`).

**Required Action:**
An administrator must resolve the password expiration issue for the remediation agent to allow these hardening steps to be successfully applied to the system.

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation attempt was unsuccessful due to a system-level restriction: the user account's password has expired, which prevents the execution of shell commands (as evidenced by the `stderr` message: `"You are required to change your password immediately (password expired)."`).

While I attempted to follow the approved plan to harden the SSH configuration by setting `X11UseLocalhost yes` in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf`, the commands failed to complete because the shell environment was interrupted by the password expiration policy.

**Attempted Remediation Steps:**
1.  **Directory Creation**: Created `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/`.
2.  **File Setup**: Created and set permissions (`0600`) for `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf`.
3.  **Conflict Removal**: Attempted to remove existing `X11UseLocalhost` entries from `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` using `sed`.
4.  **Configuration Application**: Attempted to write `X11UseLocalhost yes` to the drop-in file.
5.  **Validation & Activation**: Attempted to validate the SSH configuration syntax (`sshd -t`) and reload the service (`systemctl reload sshd`).

**Required Action:**
An administrator must resolve the password expiration issue for the remediation agent to allow these hardening steps to be successfully applied to the system.

review_verdict.feedback: The proposed plan is excellent and directly addresses all concerns raised in the previous review. It now includes a syntax validation step (sshd -t) and a service reload (systemctl reload sshd), which mitigates the risk of lockout and ensures the changes take effect.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring 'X11UseLocalhost yes' is set via a configuration drop-in file. Crucially, it includes 'sshd -t' to validate syntax before reloading the service, which prevents accidental lockouts due to configuration errors.

vulnerability.title: Certificate status checking in SSSD
vulnerability.description: Multifactor solutions that require devices separate from information systems gaining access include, for example, hardware tokens providing time-based or challenge-response authenticators and smart cards. Configuring certificate_verification to ocsp_dgst= ensures that certificates for multifactor solutions are checked via Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP).
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q sssd-common; then var_sssd_certificate_verification_digest_function='' # sssd configuration files must be created with 600 permissions if they don't exist # otherwise the sssd module fails to start OLD_UMASK=$(umask) umask u=rw,go= MAIN_CONF="/etc/sssd/conf.d/certificate_verification.conf" found=false # set value in all files if they contain section or key for f in $(echo -n "$MAIN_CONF /etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/conf.d/*.conf"); do if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then continue fi # find key in section and change value if grep -qzosP "[[:space:]]*\[sssd\]([^\n\[]*\n+)+?[[:space:]]*certificate_verification" "$f"; then if ! grep -qPz "certificate_verification=ocsp_dgst=$var_sssd_certificate_verification_digest_function" "$f"; then

triage.reason: The remediation involves modifying SSSD configuration files to enforce OCSP certificate verification. While this is a standard hardening step, misconfiguration of SSSD or issues with the OCSP responder availability can lead to authentication failures for users relying on smart cards or certificates, potentially locking out users from the system.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; networking affected; Potential for authentication lockout if OCSP responder is unreachable; SSSD service restart required to apply changes; Risk of breaking existing multifactor authentication workflows

vulnerability.title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to verify the certificate of the user or group. To set this up ensure that section like certmap/testing.test/rule_name is setup in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example [certmap/testing.test/rule_name] matchrule =<SAN>.*EDIPI@mil maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin}) domains = testing.test
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Certmap in SSSD

triage.reason: Enabling Certmap in SSSD involves configuring specific certificate mapping rules in sssd.conf. Incorrect configuration of these rules can lead to authentication failures for users or groups relying on certificate-based identity, potentially causing a lockout from the system or critical services.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect matchrule or maprule syntax can break authentication; Misconfiguration may prevent valid users from accessing the system; Requires existing knowledge of the specific certificate attributes and domain structure

vulnerability.title: Enable Smartcards in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to authenticate access to the system using smart cards. To enable smart cards in SSSD, set pam_cert_auth to True under the [pam] section in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example: [pam] pam_cert_auth = True Add or update "pam_sss.so" line in auth section of "/etc/pam.d/system-auth" file to include "try_cert_auth" or "require_cert_auth" option, like in the following example: /etc/pam.d/system-auth:auth [success=done authinfo_unavail=ignore ignore=ignore default=die] pam_sss.so try_cert_auth Also add or update "pam_sss.so" line in auth section of "/etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth" file to include the "allow_missing_name" option, like in the following example: /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth:auth sufficient pam_sss.so allow_missing_name
vulnerability.recommendation:
Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q sssd-common; then # sssd configuration files must be created with 600 permissions if they don't exist # otherwise the sssd module fails to start OLD_UMASK=$(umask) umask u=rw,go= found=false # set value in all files if they contain section or key for f in $(echo -n "/etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/conf.d/*.conf"); do if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then continue fi # find key in section and change value if grep -qzosP "[[:space:]]*\[pam\]([^
\[]*
+)+?[[:space:]]*pam_cert_auth" "$f"; then if ! grep -qPz "pam_cert_auth=True" "$f"; then sed -i "s/pam_cert_auth[^(
)]*/pam_cert_auth=True/" "$f" fi found=true # find section and add key = value to it elif grep -qs "[[:space:]]*\[pam\]" "$f"; then sed -i "/[[:space:]]*\[pam\]/a pam_cert_au

triage.reason: The remediation involves modifying PAM configuration files (/etc/pam.d/system-auth) and SSSD configuration. While this is a standard hardening pattern, improper configuration of PAM modules (specifically adding 'try_cert_auth' or 'require_cert_auth') carries a high risk of locking users out of the system if smart card hardware or middleware is not correctly configured or available.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for user lockout if PAM configuration is incorrect; Dependency on functional smart card middleware/drivers; Changes to authentication flow (PAM)
